questions.question: Part A: What is the main conflict Rachel faces in the story?

responses.content: D. She is forced to wear a sweater that isn't hers and can't effectively stand up for herself.

questions.question: Part B: Which evidence from the text BEST shows how Rachel responds to this conflict?

responses.content: A. “That's when everything I've been holding in since this morning, since when Mrs. Price put the sweater on my desk, finally lets go, and all of a sudden I'm crying in front of everybody.”

Because the sweater isn’t hers.

questions.question:
How does Rachel feel when Sylvia says the sweater belongs to her, and why does she feel this way? 

Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, evidence from paragraphs 8-11, and explanation.

responses.content: Rachel feels humiliated and uncomfortable. The reason she feels this way is because the red sweater that Sylvia claimed to belong to Rachel was “raggedy” and “1000” years old.

Rachel feels humiliated and uncomfortable. The reason she feels this way is because the red sweater that Sylvia claimed to belong to Rachel was “raggedy” and “1000” years old.Paragraph 8 says “It’s a ugly sweater” Paragraph 10 says “not mine…” this shows how raheel feels

questions.question:
Re-read the following line from paragraph 14:

“Except when math period ends Mrs. Price says loud and in front of everybody, ‘Now, Rachel, that's enough,’ because she sees I've shoved the red sweater to the tippy-tip corner of my desk and it's hanging over the edge like a waterfall, but I don't care.”

What specific characteristic do waterfalls and the hanging sweater share that makes this comparison effective?

responses.content: Waterfall

questions.question: In paragraph 19, Rachel says she's “crying like I'm three in front of everybody.” This comparison is an example of:

responses.content: the sweater was over the edge like a waterfall falling
They both hang over the edge

questions.question: How does the figurative language in paragraph 14 help readers understand Rachel's reaction to the sweater?

responses.content: A. The simile in the paragraph helps readers understand how Rachel feels about the sweater.

questions.question: How does the figurative language in paragraph 19 help readers visualize Rachel's behavior?

responses.content: C. The similes about her body shaking “like when you have the hiccups” and her head hurting “like when you drink milk too fast” connect to unpleasant experiences most readers have had.

questions.question: What does Sal learn about herself while watching this family interaction?

responses.content: Sal treated her mom with respect and just did not want to go on the walk. Phoebe treated her mom disrespectfully by screaming at her mom and slamming doors.

questions.question: Which evidence from the text best shows Prudence's reaction to the mysterious note?

responses.content: Watching this family interact showed Sal that phoebes mom was feeling sad and maybe Sals mom was feeling sad the day she wanted to go on a walk.

Prudence did not want her mom to go to cheer tryouts like Sal did not want to go on a walk with her mom. Their own agendas did not involve spending time with their mom. Sal realizes that she should’ve spent time time with her mom maybe because her mom was sad just like Phoebes mom.

questions.question:
Throughout the excerpt, Sal watches the Winterbottom family interact and then reflects on what matters, ‘in the course of a lifetime.’

According to the last paragraph, how does watching the Winterbottom family help Sal understand the difference between things that matter and things that don't matter in life? Use evidence from the text to support your response.

responses.content: Watching the Winterbottom family helps Sal understand what matters in life. One piece of evidence is paragraph 25 when Prudence screams at her mom telling her she can’t come watch tryouts, “You can’t it would be it would be awful.” Another piece of evidence is Phoebe whining about her mom giving her a kiss, on paragraph 9 it said “I’m not a baby you know.” The last piece of evidence is when Sal remembered about her mom asking to go on a walk with her. Sal refused not knowing her mom would die the next day. This shows how Sal finally understands what matters in Life.

Watching the Winterbottom family helps Sal understand what matters in life. One piece of evidence is paragraph 25 when Prudence screams at her mom telling her she can’t come watch tryouts, “You can’t it would be it would be awful.” Another piece of evidence is Phoebe whining about her mom giving her a kiss, on paragraph 9 it said “I’m not a baby you know.” The last piece of evidence is when Sal remembered about her mom asking to go on a walk with her. Sal refused not knowing her mom would die the next day. This shows how Sal finally understands what matters in Life.

Watching the Winterbottom family helps Sal understand what matters in life because it reminds her of what happened with her and her mom. One piece of evidence is paragraph 25 when Prudence screams at her mom telling her she can’t come watch tryouts, “You can’t it would be it would be awful.” Another piece of evidence is Phoebe whining about her mom giving her a kiss, on paragraph 9 it said “I’m not a baby you know.” The last piece of evidence is when Sal remembered about her mom asking to go on a walk with her. Sal refused not knowing her mom would die the next day. This shows how Sal finally understands what matters in Life.

activities.text_title: Heroes Every Child Should Know: Perseus

questions.question: Look at paragraphs 46-47. How do Perseus's actions show that he followed Athene's plan?

responses.content: He wraps up her head, doesnt

questions.question: What does Perseus's response to the Gorgons in paragraph 49 reveal about his state of mind?

responses.content: Perseus actions show us that he followed Athens’s plan because he got to Medusa and slayed her
He also used the mirror

questions.question: In paragraph 49, Perseus calls the sandals “brave.” What does this reveal about Perseus' character in this moment?

responses.content: He is turning to the sandals for help. And that they are brave. He is also feeling really scared.

questions.question: Based on Perseus's actions in paragraphs 46 through 47, the reader can conclude that—

responses.content: A. Athene will be pleased with the resolution of the plot's conflict.

questions.question: When Perseus's blood runs cold in paragraph 49, it reveals that he has—

responses.content: C. brief doubt in Athene's plan.

questions.question: Which phrase from paragraph 29 best signals the author's use of flashback?

responses.content: Percy is concerned because his mom is sending him to a new school
responses.bucket_rationale: The response accurately identifies Percy's concern as being sent away by his mom, which matches the key elements in paragraphs 24-28. The answer is specific and aligns with the text, meeting both the form and accuracy requirements for the 'correct' bucket.

questions.question: How did the author structure the flashback in paragraphs 29-32?

responses.content: B. flood of memories came back to me

questions.question: How do paragraphs 30-31 support Percy's statement?

responses.content: The author structured the flashback backwards
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'The author structured the flashback backwards' directly identifies a key structural element of the flashback: that the memories are presented in reverse chronological order (from third grade to preschool). This matches the text, which starts with a memory from third grade and then goes further back to preschool. The answer is concise but accurate, and it addresses the structure of the flashback, not just its content. Therefore, it meets the criteria for 'correct.'

questions.question: Why might the author choose to use the flashbacks in paragraphs 30 and 31?

responses.content: D. to reinforce the idea that Percy is different and unique

questions.question: Which sentence or phrase from the passage gives the clearest understanding that the story will be about Percy's struggles?

responses.content: B. “In every single school, something creepy had happened, something unsafe, and I was forced to move.”

questions.question: Reread the dialogue in paragraphs 14-16. How does learning this truth about his father affect Percy's feelings toward his father?

responses.content: He felt u
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'He felt u' is incomplete but shows an attempt to engage with Percy's feelings. It does not clearly identify Percy's negative self-view, but it is not generic or off-topic. It is text-specific and shows partial understanding, but does not meet the threshold for 'correct' because it does not specify the negative self-perception.

responses.content: Percy sees himself as different because he is dyslexic hyperactive and gets bad grades in school
responses.bucket_rationale: The response clearly identifies that Percy sees himself as different because of his dyslexia, hyperactivity, and poor grades. This matches the negative self-perception described in paragraph 12. The answer is in the student's own words and aligns with the key scene elements, meeting the threshold for 'correct.'

questions.question: Which moment in this excerpt represents the most significant turning point for Percy?

responses.content: He felt angry because his father was never there for him
responses.bucket_rationale: The response clearly identifies that Percy felt angry because his father was never there for him. This matches the key emotion (anger/resentment) depicted in the text and provides a reason for Percy's feelings, meeting both the form and accuracy requirements for the 'correct' bucket.

questions.question: Reread paragraphs 20-26 where Percy and his mother discuss being sent away. How does the author create tension in this scene?

responses.content: B. When Percy learns his father left before he was born in paragraph 16

questions.question: In paragraphs 34-38, Percy's mother mentions a summer camp but refuses to give Percy more information about it or why he must go there. What does this lack of information create for Percy?

responses.content: The author creates tension by making it seem like Percys mom ghost wants to send him away
responses.bucket_rationale: The response shows text-specific engagement by mentioning Percy's mom wanting to send him away, which is a key part of the scene. However, it does not identify a specific literary technique (such as emotional reactions, unanswered questions, or mystery) used by the author to create tension. The answer describes what happens but does not explain how the author creates tension, which is required for a 'correct' classification. There is no contradiction or fabrication, so 'incorrect' does not apply. The response is not a question, not limited effort, and not inappropriate or likely not original.

responses.content: My his mom saying I-I have to, honey. For your own good. I have to send you away.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response quotes the mother's dialogue, which shows her hesitation and emotional struggle. This is a literary technique (hesitation, emotional dialogue) that creates tension, matching the key scene elements. The answer is brief but meets the threshold for identifying a technique used by the author to create tension.

questions.question:
Percy's conversation with his mother about his father serves as a turning point in the excerpt. How does this turning point propel the plot forward and hint at challenges Percy will face? 


Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, evidence, and explanation.

responses.content: Percy conversation with his mom propels the plot because Percy gets mad at his mom because she’s trying to send him away without saying anything about it.” I'm sorry, Percy," she said, seeing the look in my eyes. "But I can't talk about it.” This shows how it propels the plot
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a claim about how the conversation propels the plot and provides a quote as evidence. However, the explanation only labels the significance ('This shows how it propels the plot') without explaining why or how the conversation moves the story forward or what challenges are hinted at. The reasoning is not fully developed, so this fits Score 2.

responses.content: Percy conversation with his mom propels the plot because Percy gets mad at his mom because she’s trying to send him away without saying anything about it.” I'm sorry, Percy," she said, seeing the look in my eyes. "But I can't talk about it.” This shows how it propels the plot. It changes the plot by making Percy mad and changing the outcome on what might happen.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a claim (the conversation propels the plot), evidence (a quote from the mother), and an explanation (Percy gets mad and it changes the outcome). However, the explanation only labels the effect ('it changes the plot by making Percy mad and changing the outcome') without explaining how or why this moves the plot forward or what specific challenges are hinted at. The response does not connect the evidence to the claim with reasoning, and it does not address the second part of the question about challenges. This fits the Score 2 pattern: foundation is present, but the explanation labels rather than connects.

questions.question: Based on paragraph 3, what can you infer about how Margaret felt about her work as a photojournalist?

responses.content: The motto her parents gave her is “you can.”
responses.bucket_rationale: The response accurately states the motto 'you can,' which matches the exact motto given in paragraph 2 of the text. The answer is brief but fully correct according to the criteria.

questions.question: Highlight ONE sentence or phrase that shows an example of when Margaret Bourke-White's hardworking attitude helped advance her career.

responses.content: Happy
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'Happy' expresses a positive feeling toward Margaret's work as a photojournalist, which aligns with the text's depiction of her as passionate, dedicated, and a trailblazer. The answer is brief but meets the threshold for expressing a positive orientation, which is sufficient for a correct classification.

questions.question: Based on paragraphs 10-12, what can you infer was important to Margaret as a photojournalist?

responses.content: Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes. Her abilities resulted in some of the best steel factory pictures of that era, and these earned her national attention.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is: "Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes. Her abilities resulted in some of the best steel factory pictures of that era, and these earned her national attention." This is within the character limit and directly answers the question by showing how Margaret's hardworking attitude (solving a technical problem and achieving success) helped advance her career. The highlighted text is also an exact match to one of the pre-approved answers.

questions.question: Based on paragraph 14, how did Margaret influence future generations of women?

responses.content: Margret wanted to bring complex events to people and make them understandable.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response directly addresses what was important to Margaret as a photojournalist, stating that she wanted to bring complex events to people and make them understandable. This matches the key scene elements from paragraphs 10-12, which describe her efforts to document and explain complex world events through her photography. The answer is accurate, text-specific, and meets the threshold for 'correct.'

questions.question: Which of the following selections best states the central idea of this biography of Margaret Bourke-White?

responses.content: D. Margaret is a great example for women because she built a successful career in a mostly male profession.

questions.question: Which statement from the text most strongly supports the answer to the previous question?

responses.content: A. “She proved to women that they had every right to pursue the careers they wanted.”
